questions.question: What is the Repository pattern and why is it used?
questions.answer:
The Repository pattern abstracts data access behind an interface — it decouples your business logic from the persistence mechanism (EF Core, Dapper, API, etc.).

public interface IPortfolioRepository
{
    Task<Portfolio?> GetByIdAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task<IReadOnlyList<Portfolio>> GetByUserAsync(string userId, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task AddAsync(Portfolio portfolio, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task DeleteAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default);
}

public class EfPortfolioRepository : IPortfolioRepository
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public EfPortfolioRepository(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    public async Task<Portfolio?> GetByIdAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default)
        => await _db.Portfolios.AsNoTracking()
               .FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id, ct);

    public async Task AddAsync(Portfolio portfolio, CancellationToken ct = default)
    {
        _db.Portfolios.Add(portfolio);
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    }
    // ...
}

Benefits: Testable (mock the interface), swappable data source, single place for all data access logic for an entity.

The controversy: EF Core's DbContext IS already a repository + Unit of Work. For small-medium apps, adding another layer is over-engineering. I use it when I need testability without hitting the real DB, or when mixing EF Core + Dapper for the same entity.

followUps.question: What is Unit of Work pattern?
followUps.answer: The Unit of Work (UoW) pattern tracks all changes made to objects during a business transaction and coordinates writing them out as a single database operation. It answers: "How do I save changes to multiple repositories in one atomic transaction?" EF Core's DbContext is the canonical Unit of Work implementation — it tracks all entity changes in memory via the change tracker and commits them all with a single SaveChangesAsync() call. If you use the Repository pattern, the Unit of Work ties them together: await _unitOfWork.CommitAsync() calls dbContext.SaveChangesAsync() internally, persisting changes from all repositories that shared the same DbContext instance.

followUps.question: Is Repository pattern needed with EF Core?
followUps.answer: Debated. Arguments against: EF Core's DbContext is already a repository (entity sets) and unit of work (SaveChanges). Adding another repository layer is extra boilerplate that wraps what EF Core already provides. EF Core can be tested directly using SQLite in-memory or mocked with MockQueryable. Arguments for: Repositories hide EF Core specifics from the domain layer — you can swap to Dapper or a different ORM without changing business logic. They provide a clear, meaningful API instead of exposing raw LINQ. They're easier to mock in unit tests than DbContext. My pragmatic take: use repositories when you need to test business logic in isolation from EF Core, or when you're mixing data sources. Skip them for simple CRUD-heavy apps.

questions.tip: Generic repository anti-pattern: IRepository<T> with just CRUD methods encourages leaking data access logic into the service layer (callers end up building queries themselves). Prefer entity-specific repositories (IPortfolioRepository) with meaningful methods.

questions.question: What is the Strategy pattern and how do you implement it in C#?
questions.answer:
The Strategy pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. The behavior can be changed at runtime without changing the class that uses it.

// Strategy interface
public interface IPaymentStrategy
{
    string Name { get; }
    Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct);
}

// Concrete strategies
public class StripePaymentStrategy : IPaymentStrategy
{
    public string Name => "stripe";
    public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    { /* Stripe API call */ }
}

public class RazorpayPaymentStrategy : IPaymentStrategy
{
    public string Name => "razorpay";
    public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    { /* Razorpay API call */ }
}

// Context that uses the strategy
public class PaymentService
{
    private readonly IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> _strategies;
    public PaymentService(IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> strategies)
        => _strategies = strategies;

    public async Task<PaymentResult> PayAsync(string provider, decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var strategy = _strategies.SingleOrDefault(s => s.Name == provider)
            ?? throw new ArgumentException($"Unknown provider: {provider}");
        return await strategy.ProcessAsync(amount, ct);
    }
}

// DI registration — all strategies injected
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentStrategy, StripePaymentStrategy>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentStrategy, RazorpayPaymentStrategy>();

followUps.question: How does Strategy differ from Factory pattern?
followUps.answer: Strategy is about choosing a behavior (algorithm) to execute at runtime — the strategies all do the same thing but differently (process payment via Stripe or Razorpay). The context holds a reference to the strategy and delegates work to it. Factory is about choosing which object to create — the factory's job ends once it returns the new instance. Factory creates; Strategy executes. They're often combined: a factory creates and returns the right strategy. Another key difference: with Strategy, the context works with the strategy through an interface at runtime; with Factory, the factory is invoked once to produce an object which is then used independently.

followUps.question: How is Strategy related to OCP?
followUps.answer: The Strategy pattern is one of the cleanest implementations of the Open/Closed Principle. The context class (PaymentService) is closed for modification — adding a new payment provider doesn't require changing it. It's open for extension — you add a new IPaymentStrategy implementation and register it in DI. The context discovers the new strategy automatically via IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> injection. Without Strategy, adding a new payment type would require modifying a switch statement in the context — a classic OCP violation. With Strategy, new behaviors extend the system without touching existing code.

questions.tip: Strategy + DI = powerful combo. Register all strategies as IEnumerable<IStrategy> and inject — the container provides all implementations. Select by a discriminator property (Name, Type, etc.). Adding a new strategy is just a new class + DI registration.

questions.question: What is the Factory pattern and its variants?
questions.answer:
Factory patterns encapsulate object creation logic.

Simple Factory — a static method or class that creates objects based on some input. Not a GoF pattern but very common.

public static class NotificationFactory
{
    public static INotification Create(string type) => type switch
    {
        "email" => new EmailNotification(),
        "sms"   => new SmsNotification(),
        _        => throw new ArgumentException($"Unknown type: {type}")
    };
}

Factory Method — defines an interface for creating objects, lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.

public abstract class ReportGenerator
{
    public abstract IReport CreateReport();  // factory method

    public void Generate()
    {
        var report = CreateReport();  // subclass decides which report
        report.Build();
        report.Export();
    }
}

public class PdfReportGenerator : ReportGenerator
{
    public override IReport CreateReport() => new PdfReport();
}

Abstract Factory — creates families of related objects. Useful for multi-platform or multi-theme scenarios.

In CSInvoice, I used a factory to create different invoice document types (PDF, Word, Excel) based on client preference — same interface, different concrete implementations selected by factory.

followUps.question: Abstract Factory vs Factory Method?
followUps.answer: Factory Method uses inheritance — a base class declares an abstract factory method that subclasses override to create the appropriate product. One factory method creates one product type. Abstract Factory uses composition — an interface declares multiple factory methods to create a family of related products. Example: a UI theme factory has methods for creating buttons, checkboxes, and dialogs — all matching the same theme (dark or light). Adding a new theme means a new concrete factory implementing all creation methods. Factory Method is simpler; Abstract Factory is for complex scenarios where you need coordinated sets of related objects.

followUps.question: When would you use Factory vs Strategy?
followUps.answer: Factory when the problem is: "Which object should I create?" — the factory's output is a new object. The choice of what to create may be based on configuration, environment, or runtime input. Strategy when the problem is: "Which algorithm should I run?" — the strategies are pre-created objects (often singletons) that are selected and executed. A common combination: a Factory creates and returns the appropriate Strategy. For example, a payment factory creates a StripePaymentStrategy based on config, then injects it where needed. If the strategies are stateless services, use DI injection of IEnumerable<IStrategy> instead of a factory — it's simpler and the container manages lifetime.

questions.tip: Factory vs Strategy: Factory is about creation (what to instantiate). Strategy is about behavior (how to do something). If you're choosing an algorithm to run, use Strategy. If you're choosing an object to create, use Factory.

questions.question: What is the Decorator pattern and how do you use it in .NET?
questions.answer:
The Decorator pattern wraps an object with additional behavior without modifying the original class. It's an alternative to inheritance for adding responsibilities.

// Base interface
public interface IEmailSender
{
    Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body);
}

// Real implementation
public class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender
{
    public async Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body)
    { /* SMTP logic */ }
}

// Logging decorator — wraps any IEmailSender
public class LoggingEmailSender : IEmailSender
{
    private readonly IEmailSender _inner;
    private readonly ILogger _logger;

    public LoggingEmailSender(IEmailSender inner, ILogger<LoggingEmailSender> logger)
    {
        _inner = inner;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Sending email to {To}: {Subject}", to, subject);
        await _inner.SendAsync(to, subject, body);
        _logger.LogInformation("Email sent successfully to {To}", to);
    }
}

// With Scrutor library for clean DI decoration
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
builder.Services.Decorate<IEmailSender, LoggingEmailSender>();

The Decorator pattern is how I add retry logic, caching, and logging to services without modifying them — perfect for the Open/Closed Principle.

followUps.question: How does Scrutor simplify Decorator registration?
followUps.answer: Without Scrutor, manually registering a decorator requires resolving the inner service: services.AddScoped<IEmailSender>(sp => new LoggingEmailSender(sp.GetRequiredService<SmtpEmailSender>(), sp.GetRequiredService<ILogger<LoggingEmailSender>>())) — verbose and error-prone. Scrutor (NuGet) adds a Decorate<TService, TDecorator>() extension that does this automatically: services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>(); services.Decorate<IEmailSender, LoggingEmailSender>();. Scrutor replaces the existing IEmailSender registration with the decorator and passes the original implementation as the inner dependency. Stack multiple decorators by calling Decorate multiple times — they wrap in declaration order.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Decorator and Proxy pattern?
followUps.answer: Both wrap an object but with different intent. Decorator adds new behavior/responsibilities to an existing object dynamically — the client knows it's using a decorator and may stack multiple decorators. Proxy controls access to an object — it may restrict access, add caching, add lazy initialization, or provide a remote surrogate. The client usually doesn't know it's using a proxy (transparent substitution). In practice, the structural implementation is nearly identical; the difference is intent. A logging wrapper is a Decorator. A caching layer that returns cached responses before hitting the real service is a Proxy (specifically, a virtual proxy or caching proxy). A security check that gates access is a Protection Proxy.

questions.tip: Scrutor's Decorate<T>() is the cleanest way to register decorators in .NET DI. Without it, you have to manually resolve the inner service and wrap it — messy. Install Scrutor NuGet and use services.Decorate<IService, DecoratorService>().

questions.question: What is the Observer pattern and how is it implemented in C#?
questions.answer:
The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency — when one object (subject) changes state, all dependents (observers) are notified automatically.

Built-in .NET mechanisms for Observer:

  • Events + delegates (classic approach)
  • IObservable<T> / IObserver<T> (Rx.NET)
  • MediatR notifications (modern approach in .NET apps)
// MediatR notification (recommended in .NET apps)
public record InvoiceCreatedNotification(int InvoiceId, string ClientEmail)
    : INotification;

// Multiple handlers — all called when notification is published
public class SendReceiptEmailHandler : INotificationHandler<InvoiceCreatedNotification>
{
    public async Task Handle(InvoiceCreatedNotification notification, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _emailSender.SendReceiptAsync(notification.ClientEmail);
}

public class UpdateAnalyticsDashboardHandler : INotificationHandler<InvoiceCreatedNotification>
{
    public async Task Handle(InvoiceCreatedNotification notification, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _analytics.IncrementInvoiceCountAsync();
}

// Publisher
await _mediator.Publish(new InvoiceCreatedNotification(invoice.Id, invoice.ClientEmail));

MediatR decouples the publisher (InvoiceService) from subscribers (email handler, analytics handler) — neither knows the other exists.

followUps.question: What is MediatR and how does it implement CQRS?
followUps.answer: MediatR is a library that implements the Mediator pattern for in-process messaging. It has two main use cases: Requests (IRequest<T>) for CQRS commands/queries — one handler per request, returns a response. Notifications (INotification) for the Observer pattern — multiple handlers, no return value. For CQRS: commands are IRequest<T> with a single handler that performs the write; queries are also IRequest<T> with a handler that returns data. The controller just calls await _mediator.Send(new GetPortfoliosQuery(userId)) — it doesn't know about handlers, DbContext, or business logic. MediatR also supports pipeline behaviors (IPipelineBehavior<TRequest, TResponse>) for cross-cutting concerns like validation, logging, and caching.

followUps.question: IObservable vs events — when to use which?
followUps.answer: Events (delegates) are simpler and sufficient for most in-process observer scenarios — a button click, a property changed notification, a domain event. They're part of the language with first-class syntax (+=, -=). IObservable<T> (Rx.NET) is the choice for observable streams of data over time — when you need to compose, filter, transform, throttle, or combine multiple streams. Example: combining keyboard input with HTTP results and debouncing is natural with Rx operators but painful with events. In Angular/frontend, RxJS (JavaScript Rx) is used extensively. In .NET backends, most developers use events/MediatR for notifications and reserve Rx for reactive data pipeline scenarios.

questions.tip: MediatR Publish is synchronous by default — all handlers run in sequence. For truly async fan-out (fire-and-forget to multiple handlers), use a message bus (Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ) instead. MediatR is for in-process notifications.

questions.question: What is CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)?
questions.answer:
CQRS separates read operations (queries) from write operations (commands) into distinct paths. Queries return data and don't change state. Commands change state and may or may not return data.

Why separate them? Read and write requirements are often different: reads need to be fast and denormalized (reporting views, dashboards), writes need validation and business rules. Separating allows independent scaling and optimization.

// Command (write) — via MediatR
public record CreatePortfolioCommand(string UserId, string Name) : IRequest<int>;

public class CreatePortfolioHandler : IRequestHandler<CreatePortfolioCommand, int>
{
    public async Task<int> Handle(CreatePortfolioCommand cmd, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // Validation, business rules, then persist
        var portfolio = new Portfolio { UserId = cmd.UserId, Name = cmd.Name };
        _db.Portfolios.Add(portfolio);
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
        return portfolio.Id;
    }
}

// Query (read) — via MediatR
public record GetPortfoliosQuery(string UserId) : IRequest<List<PortfolioDto>>;

public class GetPortfoliosHandler : IRequestHandler<GetPortfoliosQuery, List<PortfolioDto>>
{
    public async Task<List<PortfolioDto>> Handle(GetPortfoliosQuery query, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // No EF tracking, straight to DTO
        return await _db.Portfolios
            .AsNoTracking()
            .Where(p => p.UserId == query.UserId)
            .Select(p => new PortfolioDto(p.Id, p.Name, p.TotalValue))
            .ToListAsync(ct);
    }
}

followUps.question: Does CQRS require Event Sourcing?
followUps.answer: No — CQRS and Event Sourcing are independent patterns that are often combined but don't require each other. CQRS separates reads from writes. Event Sourcing stores the history of domain events as the source of truth instead of the current state — you rebuild current state by replaying events. They combine well: commands produce events (Event Sourcing), events update a separate read model (CQRS). But you can use CQRS with a regular relational database (just different code paths for reads/writes), and you can use Event Sourcing without CQRS. CQRS alone is a simple and low-risk pattern; adding Event Sourcing significantly increases complexity and should only be done when the audit trail or time-travel query benefits are worth it.

followUps.question: What is the read model in CQRS?
followUps.answer: In full CQRS with separate databases, the read model (or read store) is a separate, denormalized data store optimized for queries. When a command changes the write model (normalized relational DB), it publishes an event that updates the read model (often a NoSQL store, materialized view, or Redis cache with pre-joined data). Queries hit the read model directly — no joins, no complex queries, just a simple lookup of pre-computed data. The trade-off: read models are eventually consistent (there's a small lag between the write and the read model update). For most applications, this eventual consistency is acceptable and the performance gain is significant. I haven't needed a separate read store in my projects — a single DB with AsNoTracking queries and proper indexes is sufficient.

questions.tip: CQRS doesn't require separate databases or microservices. Even in a monolith, separating command handlers (EF Core with full tracking) from query handlers (Dapper or AsNoTracking) is valuable. Start simple — you can add separate read stores later if needed.

questions.question: What is the Singleton pattern and how is it different from Singleton lifetime in DI?
questions.answer:
The Singleton design pattern ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point. Classic implementation:

// Thread-safe Singleton (classic pattern)
public sealed class Configuration
{
    private static readonly Lazy<Configuration> _instance =
        new Lazy<Configuration>(() => new Configuration());

    private Configuration() { /* private constructor */ }

    public static Configuration Instance => _instance.Value;

    public string ApiKey { get; private set; } = string.Empty;
}

Problems with classic Singleton:

  • Global state — makes testing hard (can't swap for test double)
  • Hides dependencies — Configuration.Instance can be called from anywhere
  • Thread safety requires careful implementation

DI Singleton lifetime — register a service as Singleton in the DI container. One instance created and reused for the app lifetime. But unlike the classic pattern:

  • The class itself has no special code — just a regular class
  • Instance is managed and disposed by the container
  • Swappable in tests (inject different implementation)
  • Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit

Prefer DI Singleton over classic Singleton pattern. I only use classic Singleton for code that truly needs to be accessible without DI (rare).

followUps.question: What is the difference between Singleton and static class?
followUps.answer: A static class cannot be instantiated at all — all members must be static. It's essentially a container for utility functions. No state management, no interfaces, no polymorphism. Singleton is an instantiated object (one instance) that can implement interfaces, be injected, be mocked in tests, and have instance state. Use static classes for stateless utility methods (Math.Sqrt, string helpers). Use Singleton (via DI) for services that need to share state across the app lifetime (IMemoryCache, a database connection pool, a configuration reader). The key practical difference: static classes can't be substituted in tests; Singleton services can be replaced with mocks via DI.

followUps.question: Thread safety of Singleton services in ASP.NET Core?
followUps.answer: Singleton services in ASP.NET Core are shared across all concurrent requests — multiple threads will access the same instance simultaneously. This means Singleton services must be thread-safe. Avoid mutable state in Singletons unless protected by locks or concurrent collections. IMemoryCache is thread-safe (designed for Singleton). A custom cache backed by a plain Dictionary<K,V> is not — use ConcurrentDictionary<K,V> instead. Also avoid injecting Scoped or Transient services into Singletons — they become effectively Singleton (captive dependency). The DI container will throw an exception at startup if you accidentally inject a Scoped service into a Singleton when scope validation is enabled.

questions.tip: Singleton services must be thread-safe in ASP.NET Core — multiple requests run concurrently and share the same instance. Avoid mutable state in singletons. If you need per-request state, use Scoped services.

questions.question: What is Clean Architecture? How do you structure a .NET project using it?
questions.answer:
Clean Architecture (by Robert Martin) is a layered approach where outer layers depend on inner layers, never the reverse. The inner layers (Domain, Application) know nothing about frameworks, databases, or UI — they're pure business logic.

The four layers (inside out):

  1. Domain — entities, value objects, domain events, interfaces. Zero external dependencies.
  2. Application — use cases/services, DTOs, command/query handlers (CQRS), validation. Depends only on Domain.
  3. Infrastructure — EF Core DbContext, file system, email services, external APIs. Depends on Application interfaces.
  4. Presentation — ASP.NET Core controllers, Razor views, API endpoints. Depends on Application.
Solution structure:
MyApp.Domain/          — Entities, Interfaces, ValueObjects
MyApp.Application/     — Services, DTOs, Validators, Handlers
MyApp.Infrastructure/  — EF Core, Repositories, Email, Azure
MyApp.Web/             — Controllers, Views, Program.cs
MyApp.Tests/           — Unit + Integration tests
// Domain layer — no framework deps
public class Order
{
    public int Id { get; private set; }
    public decimal Total { get; private set; }
    public void ApplyDiscount(decimal percent) { ... } // domain logic
}

// Application layer — defines the contract
public interface IOrderRepository
{
    Task<Order?> GetByIdAsync(int id);
    Task SaveAsync(Order order);
}

// Application service — use case
public class PlaceOrderService
{
    private readonly IOrderRepository _repo;
    private readonly IEmailService _email;

    public PlaceOrderService(IOrderRepository repo, IEmailService email)
    {
        _repo = repo; _email = email;
    }

    public async Task PlaceAsync(PlaceOrderDto dto)
    {
        var order = new Order(dto.Items);
        await _repo.SaveAsync(order);
        await _email.SendConfirmationAsync(order.Id);
    }
}

// Infrastructure — implements the interface with EF Core
public class EfOrderRepository : IOrderRepository
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _ctx;
    public EfOrderRepository(AppDbContext ctx) => _ctx = ctx;
    public async Task<Order?> GetByIdAsync(int id) => await _ctx.Orders.FindAsync(id);
    public async Task SaveAsync(Order order)
    {
        _ctx.Orders.Add(order);
        await _ctx.SaveChangesAsync();
    }
}

// Web/Program.cs — wires everything up
builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderRepository, EfOrderRepository>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<PlaceOrderService>();

Key benefits I've seen: Application services are testable in isolation (mock IOrderRepository), you can swap EF Core for Dapper without touching business logic, and UI changes don't cascade into domain code.

Common tool stack: MediatR for CQRS in Application layer, FluentValidation for input validation, AutoMapper for DTO mapping.
followUps: How does Clean Architecture differ from just MVC with a service layer?
Where does MediatR fit in Clean Architecture?
What is the Dependency Inversion Principle and how does Clean Architecture enforce it?
questions.tip: The Dependency Rule: source code dependencies point inward only. Domain has zero external dependencies. Infrastructure implements interfaces defined in Application. This makes domain and application logic testable without touching a database or HTTP.
